title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who learned traditional pottery. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: The Potter’s Wheel: Shaping Clay and Life

texts.C:
### Marco, The Professional Transition
'I spent years in digital design, so feeling the clay was a shock,' Marco says. [51]The most difficult part was the physical strength required in the arms and wrists, which I hadn't expected at all.[/51] [48]I was surprised to find that my chronic neck pain from computer work completely disappeared once I corrected my posture at the wheel.[/48] [53]I learned that you can't force the clay; you have to work with its moisture and speed, which is a lesson in humility.[/53] [50]I realized that my background in 3D modeling helped me understand the symmetry and volume of the pieces much faster.[/50] [47]Initially, the mess and the constant dust were quite disorienting for someone used to a clean office.[/47] It was a grounding transformation.
texts.D:
### Sarah, The Weekend Workshop
Sarah took a course to escape her stressful job. 'It was my therapy,' she explains. [49]The experience taught me that it’s important to have something in your life that you do purely for the joy of it, not for profit.[/49] [52]I hadn't anticipated the deep sense of pride I’d feel when drinking from a mug I’d made with my own hands.[/52] [47]I was quite startled by how much the cold temperature of the clay affected my joints during the first few sessions.[/47] [54]What I valued most was the patience of the teacher, who reminded me that every 'ruined' pot was just more practice.[/54] [56]If I hadn't seen an advertisement in a local café, I never would have signed up.[/56] It was a creative breakthrough.
texts.B:
### Victor, The Traditional Kiln Build
Victor helped build a wood-fired kiln in the countryside. 'It’s a primal process,' he notes. [51]We struggled with the constant need to monitor the temperature for 48 hours straight, which was physically and mentally exhausting.[/51] [48]I noticed that my general focus and ability to stay calm under pressure reached an all-time high, which was a great side effect.[/48] [53]I realized that the fire and the smoke give each piece a unique fingerprint that no electric kiln could ever replicate.[/53] [50]I found that the discipline I used in my previous career as a fire captain was perfect for managing the kiln safely.[/50] [55]It took a while to adjust to the isolation of the rural workshop, which felt quite lonely at first.[/55] [52]I’d initially worried the work would be too dangerous, but the teamwork was excellent.
texts.A:
### Elena, The Sculptural Approach
'I wanted to move beyond functional pots,' Elena laughs. [49]The project made me realize that my perfectionism was actually a barrier to my creative growth.[/49] [56]I wouldn't have found the studio if my sister hadn't gifted me a set of clay tools for my birthday.[/56] [54]I was moved by the camaraderie among the other students, who were always willing to share their glaze recipes.[/54] [47]I hadn't expected the physical exhaustion of standing for hours while sculpting to be so draining initially.[/47] [55]I found the initial learning curve of understanding chemical glaze reactions incredibly confusing and frustrating.[/55] [52]I’d been concerned that I wasn't artistic enough, but the clay was very forgiving.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by the physical or environmental impact of the pottery process?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) was disoriented by the mess/dust, Sarah (B) by the cold clay, and Elena (D) by the exhaustion of standing.

questions.question: notes a positive physical or mental health change that resulted from the craft?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) saw his neck pain disappear, and Victor (C) reached an all-time high in focus/calm.

questions.question: expresses a change in their outlook regarding joy, profit, or personal perfectionism?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) learned to do things for joy not profit, and Elena (D) saw her perfectionism as a barrier.

questions.question: mentions how their professional background or discipline was advantageous to the learning?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) used 3D modeling skills, and Victor (C) used his fire captain experience.

questions.question: describes a specific physical or endurance challenge during the process?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) mentions the strength required in arms/wrists, and Victor (C) the 48-hour kiln monitoring.

questions.question: admits to having had prior concerns or having a strong emotional reaction to their own work?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) felt deep pride in her mug, and Elena (D) worried she wasn't artistic enough.

questions.question: reflects on a broader philosophical lesson learned about working with the material or the kiln?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) learned humility from the clay, and Victor (C) saw the kiln's unique fingerprint on pieces.

questions.question: highlights the value of the guidance or the sharing of knowledge among others?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) valued the teacher's patience, and Elena (D) the sharing of glaze recipes.

questions.question: describes a period of frustration or adjustment to the social or technical aspects?
questions.explanation: Victor (C) adjusted to the isolation, and Elena (D) found glaze chemistry confusing.

questions.question: acknowledges the specific external factor or gift that led to their involvement?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) saw an ad in a café, and Elena (D) was gifted tools by her sister.
